(formerly known as the **Sawai Gandharva Sangeet Mahotsav**[[1]](#cite_note-1) and simply known as **Sawai**) is an annual [Indian Classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_Classical_Music "Hindustani Classical Music") festival held in [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune") since 1953. The festival is hosted by the Arya Sangeet Prasarak Mandal (ASPM) and initiated by [Bhimsen Joshi](/wiki/Bhimsen_Joshi "Bhimsen Joshi") as a memorial music conference commemorating the life and work of Joshi's [guru](/wiki/Guru "Guru"), [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), the festival's [namesake](/wiki/Namesake "Namesake").

Archived from [the original](http://www.hindu.com/thehindu/mp/2002/10/31/stories/2002103100070100.htm) on 19 October 2003. Retrieved 27 April 2013.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indian classical music festival

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival "Special:EditPage/Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Festival") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Festival"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Sawai+Gandharva+Bhimsen+Festival%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Sawai+Gandharva+Bhimsen+Festival%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Sawai+Gandharva+Bhimsen+Festival%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Sawai+Gandharva+Bhimsen+Festival%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Sawai+Gandharva+Bhimsen+Festival%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Sawai+Gandharva+Bhimsen+Festival%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Mahotsav | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Dates | Traditionally the second weekend of December |
| Locations | formerly New English School Ramanbaug,  2018 - Maharashtraiya Sankul, Mukund Nagar  [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune"), Maharashtra, India |
| Years active | 1953 – present |
| Founders | [Bhimsen Joshi](/wiki/Bhimsen_Joshi "Bhimsen Joshi"),  [Arya Sangeet Prasarak Mandal](/wiki/Arya_Sangeet_Prasarak_Mandal "Arya Sangeet Prasarak Mandal") |
| Website | [sawaigandharvabhimsenmahotsav.com](http://sawaigandharvabhimsenmahotsav.com/) |

The **Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Mahotsav** (formerly known as the **Sawai Gandharva Sangeet Mahotsav**[[1]](#cite_note-1) and simply known as **Sawai**) is an annual [Indian Classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_Classical_Music "Hindustani Classical Music") festival held in [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune") since 1953. The festival is hosted by the Arya Sangeet Prasarak Mandal (ASPM) and initiated by [Bhimsen Joshi](/wiki/Bhimsen_Joshi "Bhimsen Joshi") as a memorial music conference commemorating the life and work of Joshi's [guru](/wiki/Guru "Guru"), [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), the festival's [namesake](/wiki/Namesake "Namesake").

While Joshi was leading the organisation of the festival, performing artists received personal invitations from him to perform at it. With an emphasis on [Hindustani Classical](/wiki/Hindustani_Classical_music "Hindustani Classical music") [Khayal music](/wiki/Khayal "Khayal"), instrumentalists, [dancers](/wiki/Indian_Classical_dance "Indian Classical dance"), [dhrupadiyas](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad"), [Bhakti musicians](/wiki/Bhajans "Bhajans"), [Ghazal musicians](/wiki/Ghazal "Ghazal"), [Qawwals](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali"), and [Carnatic musicians](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") frequent the concert lineup.

A younger artist's debut performance at the festival connotes their "arrival" and promise to the Classical music scene of India (most recently including [Rahul Deshpande](/wiki/Rahul_Deshpande "Rahul Deshpande"), [Kaushiki Chakrabarty](/wiki/Kaushiki_Chakrabarty "Kaushiki Chakrabarty"), and [Mahesh Kale](/wiki/Mahesh_Kale "Mahesh Kale")).

Since its inception, the festival has grown into a prominent cultural soiree for Pune's musical connoisseurs, featuring the foremost musicians of traditional Indian musical forms.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The festival was started in 1953 on a small scale, to commemorate the first death anniversary of [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), the acclaimed Hindustani Classical vocalist of the [Kirana Gharana](/wiki/Kirana_Gharana "Kirana Gharana").

Bhimsen Joshi led the organisation of the festival from its beginning until 2002 when his health problems became of concern.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Joshi's youngest son, [Shrinivas Joshi](/wiki/Shrinivas_Joshi "Shrinivas Joshi"), and senior disciple, Shrikant Deshpande, succeeded him as organisers.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Since Joshi's death in 2011, Shrinivas Joshi has been the main organiser.

## Recent years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Recent years")]

The 2009 Festival was postponed to January 2010 due to an [H1N1](/wiki/H1N1 "H1N1") scare in Pune.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Heavy rain on 13 December 2014 caused the festival to be postponed until 1 January 2015.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

When Bhimsen Joshi died on 24 January 2011, the ASPM decided to rename the festival from its original name of 'Sawai Gandharva Mahotsav' to 'Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Mahotsav' in his honour.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Tradition")]

The Sawai Gandharva Music Festival is conducted every year in the first two weeks of December over three days. In the past, the festival runs through whole the night and the days used to end in the morning. Later on, when [Pune Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Pune_Municipal_Corporation "Pune Municipal Corporation") set rule to finish the program before 10 pm which leads to change in the schedule of the program. Traditionally, each festival is inaugurated with a tribute honoring [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), with a [Shehnai](/wiki/Shehnai "Shehnai") musician performing first, in the early morning. During the first two days, programs begin in the late-afternoon and are supposed to officially end by 10 pm. On the third and final day (previously a Saturday, now usually a Sunday), the programme is conducted in two sessions, the first beginning in the early morning continuing until the early afternoon, the second session begins in the early evening and ends at 10 p.m.

### Finale performances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Finale performances")]

Tradition was that the finale performance was presented by Bhimsen Joshi. Since his retirement, other members of the Kirana Gharana took on the role, including [Sangmeshwar Gurav](/wiki/Sangmeshwar_Gurav "Sangmeshwar Gurav") in 2005 and leading disciples of Joshi in 2006. The vocalist [Prabha Atre](/wiki/Prabha_Atre "Prabha Atre") has concluded every festival since 2007.

The festival officially concludes with the entire audience and remaining performers listening to the [Thumri](/wiki/Thumri "Thumri") [in Raag Bhairavi](/wiki/Bhairavi_(Hindustani) "Bhairavi (Hindustani)") recording "Jamuna Ke Teer" by [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), a tune made famous by and signature of his guru, Abdul Karim Khan.

## Accompanists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Accompanists")]

* [Pt.Appasaheb Jalgaonkar](/wiki/Appa_Jalgaonkar "Appa Jalgaonkar") (born 1922): 1952–2009
* [Pt. Arawind Thatte](/wiki/Arwind_Thatte "Arwind Thatte") (born 1958): 1990–present
* [Pt. Purushottam Walawalkar](/wiki/Purshottam_Walawalkar "Purshottam Walawalkar")
* [Ustad Shaik Dawood](/wiki/Shaikh_Dawood_Khan "Shaikh Dawood Khan") (born 1916) 1953–1988
* [Tulsidas Borkar](/wiki/Tulsidas_Borkar "Tulsidas Borkar") (born 1934) 1989-2006
* [Sudhir Nayak](/wiki/Sudhir_Nayak "Sudhir Nayak") (born 1972) 1992-present
